conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions. Let me start by reading the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have two children - Alex, who's 8, and Blake, who's 14.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you have two children - Alex (age 8) and Blake (age 14).

Now, could you please tell me: **Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. So you live in the UK with your two children, Alex (8) and Blake (14).

Next question: **Do both Alex and Blake live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect. Now I need to ask: **Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

**Are either Alex or Blake currently in hospital, care, or residential accommodation for an extended period?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in hospital or care.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. One final question to complete the assessment:

**Has Blake (your 14-year-old) started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or started claiming any benefits in their own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake doesn't work and isn't claiming any benefits.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I can now assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Let me summarize your situation:
- You live in the UK (Manchester)
- You have two children: Alex (8) and Blake (14)
- Both children live with you
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child
- Neither child is in hospital or care
- Blake is not working 24+ hours per week or claiming benefits in their own right

Based on the official UK government guidance, you meet all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for both children.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.**

You qualify because:
- You live in the UK and are responsible for both children
- Both children are under 16 years old
- Both children live with you
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them

**Important note:** Please be aware that if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge. However, this doesn't affect your eligibility to claim - you can still claim Child Benefit but may need to pay some or all of it back through your tax return.

If you haven't already claimed, you can apply for Child Benefit through the official government website or by contacting HMRC.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), lives with the claimant in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together. Alex is not in hospital or care for an extended period. All eligibility criteria under UK Child Benefit rules are met.

Blake is 14 years old (under 16), lives with the claimant in the UK, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. The claimant is responsible for Blake as they live together. Blake is not in hospital or care, has not started paid work for 24+ hours per week, and is not claiming benefits in their own right. All eligibility criteria under UK Child Benefit rules are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with two children: Alex (8) and Blake (14). Both children live with the claimant, are under 16 years old, are not in hospital or care, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. Blake has not started working 24+ hours per week or claiming benefits. The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for both children.
